words.definition: An unfair situation in which some people have more opportunities, money, or power than others.
words.example: Income inequality has grown significantly over the past four decades in most developed countries.

words.definition: Treated as insignificant or peripheral, excluded from mainstream social, economic, or political life.
words.example: Marginalised communities often face compounded barriers to education and healthcare.

words.definition: A system in which power and status are assigned on the basis of individual ability or achievement, rather than wealth or class.
words.example: A genuine meritocracy requires equal access to opportunity from the earliest years of life.

words.definition: The division of society into hierarchical layers, typically based on wealth, class, or status.
words.example: Social stratification based on wealth and class is reinforced by selective educational institutions.

words.definition: Deprived of a right or privilege, especially the right to vote or to participate meaningfully in society.
words.example: Many young voters feel politically disenfranchised by a system that rarely addresses their concerns.

words.definition: Active participation by individuals in the political and social life of their community.
words.example: Civic engagement has declined significantly among younger generations in many democracies.

words.definition: Relating to policies designed to transfer wealth or resources from some groups to others, typically to reduce inequality.
words.example: Addressing inequality requires both redistributive fiscal policy and investment in public services.

words.definition: The degree to which members of a society feel connected to, and trust, one another.
words.example: Growing income inequality undermines social cohesion and weakens democratic participation.

questionGroups.instructions: Complete each sentence below. Choose ONE WORD OR PHRASE from the box for each answer.
questionGroups.summaryText:
Income ___1___ has grown significantly over the past four decades in most developed countries.

___2___ communities often face compounded barriers to education and healthcare.

A genuine ___3___ requires equal access to opportunity from the earliest years of life.

Social ___4___ based on wealth and class is reinforced by selective educational institutions.

Many young voters feel politically ___5___ by a system that rarely addresses their concerns.

___6___ has declined significantly among younger generations in many democracies.

Addressing inequality requires both ___7___ fiscal policy and investment in public services.

Growing income inequality undermines ___8___ and weakens democratic participation.

words.definition: Systematic errors in AI outputs that disadvantage particular groups, caused by biased training data or flawed design.
words.example: Algorithmic bias in facial recognition disproportionately misidentifies people of colour.

words.definition: The ability to effectively find, evaluate, use, and create information using digital technology.
words.example: Digital literacy is now as fundamental a skill as reading and writing in the modern economy.

words.definition: An economic system centred on the commodification of personal data collected through monitoring users' behaviour.
words.example: Surveillance capitalism turns users' behavioural data into a commodity sold to advertisers.

words.definition: An innovation that significantly displaces an established technology or fundamentally changes an industry.
words.example: Disruptive technology destroys existing business models before viable alternatives have emerged.

words.definition: The use of largely automatic equipment in a system of manufacturing or other production process, reducing the need for human labour.
words.example: Automation threatens routine cognitive and physical labour across many sectors of the economy.

words.definition: The gap between those who have easy access to digital technology and the internet, and those who do not.
words.example: The digital divide between high- and low-income households has widened considerably since 2020.

words.definition: Relating to an owner or ownership, especially describing technology or software owned exclusively by a company.
words.example: The company's proprietary algorithm is protected as a trade secret.

words.definition: Capable of being expanded or adapted to handle a growing amount of work or a larger scope.
words.example: Investors were drawn to the startup's highly scalable business model.

questionGroups.instructions: Complete each sentence below. Choose ONE WORD OR PHRASE from the box for each answer.
questionGroups.summaryText:
___1___ in facial recognition disproportionately misidentifies people of colour.

___2___ is now as fundamental a skill as reading and writing in the modern economy.

___3___ turns users' behavioural data into a commodity sold to advertisers.

___4___ destroys existing business models before viable alternatives have emerged.

___5___ threatens routine cognitive and physical labour across many sectors of the economy.

The ___6___ between high- and low-income households has widened considerably since 2020.

The company's ___7___ algorithm is protected as a trade secret.

Investors were drawn to the startup's highly ___8___ business model.

words.definition: The total amount of greenhouse gases produced directly or indirectly by an individual, organisation, or activity.
words.example: The average carbon footprint of citizens in wealthy nations far exceeds the planet's sustainable capacity.

words.definition: The misleading practice of making false or exaggerated environmental claims to appear eco-friendly.
words.example: Several major airlines have been accused of greenwashing over their carbon offset schemes.

words.definition: The variety of plant and animal life in a particular habitat or in the world as a whole.
words.example: Protecting biodiversity is essential for sustaining the ecosystem services on which human life depends.

words.definition: A state in which the greenhouse gases going into the atmosphere are balanced by their removal.
words.example: Achieving net zero by 2050 requires the fundamental transformation of energy, transport, and industry.

words.definition: The long-term storage of carbon dioxide, typically by natural processes such as forests or oceans, or by artificial means.
words.example: Natural carbon sequestration by forests and peatlands is one of the most cost-effective climate tools.

words.definition: Originating from human activity, especially referring to environmental change or pollution.
words.example: The anthropogenic causes of climate change are now beyond scientific dispute.

words.definition: Action taken to reduce the severity or seriousness of something, especially the impact of climate change.
words.example: Without aggressive climate mitigation, global temperatures could rise by 3 to 4 degrees this century.

words.definition: Relating to a natural resource or energy source that is not depleted when used, such as sunlight or wind.
words.example: Transitioning to renewable energy is the cornerstone of credible modern climate policy.

questionGroups.summaryText:
The average ___1___ of citizens in wealthy nations far exceeds the planet's sustainable capacity.

Several major airlines have been accused of ___2___ over their carbon offset schemes.

Protecting ___3___ is essential for sustaining the ecosystem services on which human life depends.

Achieving ___4___ by 2050 requires the fundamental transformation of energy, transport, and industry.

Natural ___5___ by forests and peatlands is one of the most cost-effective climate tools.

The ___6___ causes of climate change are now beyond scientific dispute.

Without aggressive climate ___7___, global temperatures could rise by 3 to 4 degrees this century.

Transitioning to ___8___ energy is the cornerstone of credible modern climate policy.
